questions.text: In healthcare fraud terminology, what is the precise definition of 'upcoding' in the context of medical billing compliance?
options: A. Billing for a higher-complexity service or procedure than what was actually provided to the patient
B. Increasing the dollar amount charged for a service without changing the CPT code submitted
C. Submitting duplicate claims for the same service across multiple payers
D. Intentionally delaying claim submission to manipulate payment timelines

questions.text: Under the Physician Self-Referral Law (Stark Law), which of the following BEST defines a 'designated health service' (DHS)?
options: A. Any medical service provided by a physician to a patient within a hospital setting
B. Specific healthcare services designated by statute (such as clinical laboratory services, imaging, and rehabilitation) for which referral restrictions apply
C. Services that require explicit prior authorization from Medicare before delivery
D. Procedures performed exclusively by board-certified specialists in accredited facilities

questions.text: What is the primary distinction between 'balance billing' and 'excessive billing' in healthcare fraud classification?
options: A. Balance billing involves charging patients the difference between provider charge and insurance payment, while excessive billing involves charging above reasonable and customary rates
B. Balance billing is always illegal, while excessive billing is acceptable if disclosed to patients in advance
C. Excessive billing applies only to Medicare claims, while balance billing applies to all payer types
D. Both terms are synonymous and used interchangeably in fraud examinations

questions.text: In healthcare fraud investigation, what does the term 'ghost employee' refer to in the context of payroll fraud?
options: A. An employee who works exclusively night shifts and is not visible during standard audits
B. An individual listed on payroll records who does not actually exist or does not perform work for the organization
C. A temporary contractor whose employment records were deleted from the system
D. An employee who splits their time between multiple healthcare facilities

questions.text: Which of the following BEST defines 'bundling' as a fraudulent billing practice in healthcare?
options: A. Combining multiple related services into a single package for insurance purposes
B. Separately billing for component parts of a single comprehensive procedure that should be billed as one bundled code
C. Grouping claims by patient demographics for statistical analysis
D. Consolidating multiple patients' records into a single master file

questions.text: In the context of healthcare fraud, what is a 'standing order' that raises compliance concerns?
options: A. A routine medical order that requires renewal by a physician every 30 days
B. An indefinite physician authorization for services without documented medical necessity or periodic review
C. A permanent order established by hospital policy for all admitted patients
D. A written request from an insurance company for pre-authorization of services

questions.text: What is the precise definition of 'false claims' under the Federal False Claims Act (31 USC §3729) in healthcare contexts?
options: A. Claims submitted with minor documentation errors or clerical mistakes
B. Claims that contain knowing or reckless misrepresentations of material fact, submitted to obtain payment
C. Claims denied by insurance companies due to coverage limitations
D. Claims submitted outside the standard billing window or with processing delays

questions.text: In healthcare fraud terminology, what does 'unbundling' specifically mean?
options: A. Removing unnecessary services from a comprehensive care package
B. Billing separately for related services that should be included in a single bundled code
C. Declining to combine separate patient cases for administrative processing
D. Returning equipment to suppliers that was previously delivered as part of a bundle

questions.text: Which of the following BEST defines a 'Stark violation' in healthcare compliance?
options: A. Any financial transaction between a physician and a healthcare facility
B. A physician's referral of designated health services to an entity with which the physician has a financial relationship, without meeting an exception
C. A hospital's refusal to grant medical privileges to a qualified physician applicant
D. A healthcare provider's failure to report suspected fraud to law enforcement

questions.text: In healthcare fraud examination, what is the technical definition of 'phantom billing'?
options: A. Billing for services provided by physicians who have retired or terminated employment
B. Billing for healthcare services that were never rendered to the patient
C. Submitting claims with incorrect patient identification numbers
D. Charging patients for experimental treatments not yet approved by the FDA

questions.text: What is the distinction between 'incident to' services and 'incident to fraud' in healthcare billing?
options: A. 'Incident to' is a legitimate billing arrangement where services provided by non-physicians are billed under a physician's credentials when specific requirements are met, while 'incident to fraud' refers to misuse of this arrangement
B. Both terms describe the same fraudulent practice of billing for services without physician oversight
C. 'Incident to' applies only to emergency services, while 'incident to fraud' applies to elective procedures
D. 'Incident to' requires government pre-approval, while 'incident to fraud' does not

questions.text: In the context of healthcare fraud, what precisely is meant by 'medically unnecessary services'?
options: A. Services that are more expensive than alternative treatment options
B. Services provided without documented clinical justification or that exceed standards of medical practice for the patient's condition
C. Services that patients decline after being fully informed of costs
D. Services not listed in the CPT coding manual as standard procedures

questions.text: Which of the following BEST defines a 'remuneration arrangement' that may trigger Anti-Kickback Statute concerns in healthcare?
options: A. Any payment made by one healthcare entity to another healthcare entity
B. A transfer of value intended to induce or reward referrals of healthcare services or purchases of healthcare items
C. Standard salary payments to employees based on established compensation schedules
D. Insurance reimbursement payments made directly to patients for out-of-pocket expenses

questions.text: A CFE is examining billing records for a radiology group and identifies that certain radiologists consistently interpret imaging studies at rates significantly higher than their peers, despite similar patient demographics and case complexity. The radiologist's interpretation patterns show no clinical justification for the variance. Which scheme is most likely being perpetrated?
options: A. Upcoding of diagnostic imaging procedures to higher-complexity CPT codes
B. Phantom billing where services were never actually performed
C. Unbundling of imaging services that should be billed as a single comprehensive code
D. Violation of Stark Law through inappropriate physician self-referral arrangements

questions.text: During an investigation of a surgical center, a CFE discovers that the facility bills for two separate surgeon fees (primary and assistant surgeon) on cases where only one surgeon performed the entire procedure. The assistant surgeon code is billed at approximately 20% of the primary surgeon's fee. How does this scheme differ from typical upcoding schemes?
options: A. It involves billing for services by individuals who were not present, rather than misrepresenting the nature of services actually rendered
B. It requires collusion between insurance companies and providers, whereas upcoding can be unilateral
C. It is more difficult to detect because it involves legitimate CPT codes applied in unauthorized combinations
D. It violates the Civil False Claims Act but not the Criminal Health Care Fraud Statute

questions.text: A CFE comparing two hospital systems with similar patient acuity levels observes that Hospital A bills significantly more Evaluation & Management (E/M) codes at the highest complexity level (99215) than Hospital B. When reviewing documentation, Hospital A's records show more extensive documentation but similar clinical decision-making complexity. This pattern suggests which distinction?
options: A. Hospital A is committing documentation fraud by creating false patient records
B. Hospital A is engaging in legitimate DRG optimization through compliant documentation practices
C. Hospital B is underbilling and leaving revenue on the table inappropriately
D. Both hospitals are equally compliant; the difference reflects legitimate variation in documentation standards

questions.text: A CFE investigating a durable medical equipment supplier identifies two distinct patterns: (1) billing for items never delivered to beneficiaries, and (2) billing for wheelchair cushions with inflated quantities where beneficiaries received only one cushion per year but claims show 12 monthly deliveries. How should these schemes be differentiated in a fraud examination report?
options: A. Pattern 1 is phantom billing; Pattern 2 is also phantom billing with higher sophistication
B. Pattern 1 is phantom billing (false claims); Pattern 2 is quantity fraud (overbilling legitimate services)
C. Pattern 1 violates the False Claims Act; Pattern 2 violates Anti-Kickback Statute provisions
D. Both patterns represent the same fraud scheme executed through different billing cycles

questions.text: A CFE discovers that a pain management clinic has established a practice where patients receive trigger point injections billed as individual injections at $400 per unit, with claims showing 8-12 injections per patient visit. Review of medical records and physician notes indicates that standard practice involves 2-3 therapeutic injections. Which fraud mechanism is most accurately characterized here?
options: A. Route of administration fraud—misrepresenting the delivery method of legitimate medications
B. Unit-of-service fraud—billing for multiple billable units when fewer units were actually administered
C. Ingredient cost manipulation—overcharging for pharmaceutical components used in injections
D. Medically unnecessary services fraud—billing for services inconsistent with medical necessity standards

questions.text: A CFE is examining claims from two different home health agencies for identical patient populations. Agency A bills predominantly for skilled nursing visits (99 visits/patient/year), while Agency B bills predominantly for physical therapy (102 visits/patient/year). Both agencies serve similar demographics. Medical necessity documentation in both cases appears adequate. What is the most appropriate investigative conclusion?
options: A. Agency A is committing fraud; Agency B is compliant because PT services are lower-cost
B. Both agencies may be compliant; variation reflects legitimate clinical judgment, but further correlation analysis with patient outcomes is warranted
C. The agencies are engaged in competitive upcoding; the higher visit count represents presumptive fraud
D. This pattern is statistically impossible and indicates phantom billing by at least one agency

questions.text: During a billing audit, a CFE discovers that a physical therapy clinic submitted claims for bilateral shoulder manipulation (CPT 98940-50) for patients with documented single-shoulder diagnoses. The provider's clinical notes support treatment of only one shoulder. This represents which specific coding violation?
options: A. Unbundling—separating a comprehensive code into component codes for higher reimbursement
B. Bilateral modifier abuse—applying bilateral service indicators when services were unilateral
C. Upcoding—billing for a higher complexity service than was actually rendered
D. Medically unnecessary service submission—billing for services lacking clinical justification

questions.text: A CFE comparing two oncology practices identifies that Practice X bills chemotherapy administration codes (96409-96417) at nearly 100% utilization rates across all patient encounters, while Practice Y shows 65-70% utilization. Both practices serve similar patient populations with comparable cancer diagnoses. Practice X's documentation is thorough and clinically appropriate. What distinction should the CFE recognize?
options: A. Practice X is fraudulently billing chemotherapy services not actually rendered
B. Practice Y is underbilling and potentially leaving legitimate revenue uncaptured
C. Higher utilization rates alone, when supported by clinical documentation, do not constitute fraud evidence
D. The 30% variance indicates that Practice X's billing is statistically impossible and requires immediate criminal referral

questions.text: A CFE investigating a hospital system discovers that an orthopedic surgeon has a financial ownership stake in an ambulatory surgery center to which he refers 87% of his patient cases for surgical procedures. The surgeon disclosed the ownership interest; the referral arrangement complies with Stark Law safe harbor provisions. However, the CFE observes that cases referred to the ASC show systematically higher surgical complexity coding compared to identical procedures performed at the hospital. What is the appropriate investigative focus?
options: A. The ownership interest itself constitutes illegal self-referral under the Stark Law
B. The referral pattern, though legally disclosed, may correlate with upcoding schemes independent of the ownership interest
C. The higher complexity coding is presumptively fraudulent because it benefits the physician's financial interest
D. This arrangement is entirely compliant; the coding variation is statistically expected across different facilities

questions.text: A CFE identifies that a hospital laboratory has implemented a requirement that all outpatient lab tests be ordered as 'comprehensive metabolic panels' rather than individual component tests, even when clinical notes indicate only specific analytes were clinically indicated. This billing pattern generates 340% more revenue per patient encounter. How should this scheme be differentiated from unbundling?
options: A. This is not unbundling; it is systematic billing for medically unnecessary services that constitute legitimate upcoding
B. This is the inverse of unbundling—forced bundling of unnecessary components into comprehensive codes for inflated reimbursement
C. This is phantom billing because the comprehensive panel was never actually performed
D. This represents appropriate compliant billing because the CPT code selected accurately describes a legitimate laboratory service

questions.text: A CFE examining claims from a sleep disorders clinic discovers that sleep studies billed as CPT 95808 (attended polysomnography) show billings for simultaneous monitoring of 8-12 patients per technician per night shift. Standard industry practice and facility technical specifications indicate maximum capacity of 2-3 simultaneous patients per technician. The clinic claims all studies were 'attended' per billing requirements. Which scheme best characterizes this violation?
options: A. Modifier fraud—incorrect use of attended versus unattended service modifiers
B. Phantom billing—services billed but never actually performed or monitored
C. Quantity fraud—billing for attended studies at volume levels physically impossible for staff capacity
D. Medically unnecessary billing—the studies themselves may be appropriate but overbilled in frequency

questions.text: A CFE is analyzing billing patterns for two competing cardiology groups offering cardiac stress testing. Group A bills predominantly for stress echocardiograms (93015) while Group B bills predominantly for nuclear stress tests (78451). Both groups serve similar patient populations and geographic areas. Group A's imaging interpretation shows higher abnormality detection rates. What represents the most appropriate investigative distinction?
options: A. Group A is committing fraud through modality selection fraud
B. Group B is underbilling by selecting lower-reimbursement modalities inappropriately

C. The choice of stress testing modality may reflect legitimate clinical judgment or patient factors; modality selection itself is not inherently fraudulent without evidence of medical inappropriateness
D. The higher abnormality detection rate in Group A constitutes presumptive evidence of overcoding

questions.text: A CFE discovers that a rehabilitation facility has implemented a billing methodology where all therapy sessions are split into two separately billable service codes with distinct CPT codes and billing lines, where a single comprehensive service code existed that accurately describes the combined therapy session. Each split generates $180 additional reimbursement per session. What is the precise technical distinction from legitimate unbundling audit findings?
options: A. This is legitimate unbundling that extracts higher reimbursement from appropriate component billing codes
B. This is intentional splitting of a unitary service into false component parts (artificial unbundling) to artificially increase reimbursement through code multiplication
C. This represents appropriate coding variation that auditors should accept as standard therapy billing practice
D. This is medically necessary service billing that justifies higher overall reimbursement regardless of code structure

questions.text: A CFE is examining billing records from an oncology practice and discovers that chemotherapy administration codes (CPT 96413-96417) are consistently billed at the highest complexity level (96417) for all patients regardless of infusion duration or clinical complexity. The practice's documentation shows most infusions lasted 15-20 minutes. What is the most likely fraud indicator present?
options: A. Unbundling of chemotherapy codes to maximize reimbursement
B. Upcoding of chemotherapy administration services based on false complexity claims
C. Billing for chemotherapy drugs that were never dispensed to patients
D. Phantom billing where chemotherapy services were not rendered at all

questions.text: During an investigation of a dialysis center, a CFE discovers that the facility submitted claims for ESRD (End-Stage Renal Disease) treatments but the center's patient roster shows only 28 active dialysis patients during a period when 47 different patients appear in billing records. Medical records for the additional 19 patients do not exist or show discharge dates prior to the service dates billed. What type of fraud is most evident?
options: A. Billing for medically unnecessary dialysis sessions
B. Phantom patient fraud and false claims submission
C. Improper cost allocation between Medicare and private payers
D. Over-billing for dialysis supplies not actually provided

questions.text: A CFE analyzing expenditures discovers that a behavioral health clinic has established a referral arrangement with a psychiatric hospital whereby the clinic receives $300 per referral. The clinic's referral rate for psychiatric hospitalizations has increased from 8% to 31% of all patient encounters over 18 months, yet clinical outcomes data shows no improvement. What compliance concern is most critical?
options: A. Violation of patient privacy and HIPAA regulations regarding referral disclosures
B. Potential Anti-Kickback Statute violation through payment-contingent referral arrangements
C. Failure to maintain adequate clinical documentation standards
D. Improper delegation of medical decision-making authority

questions.text: An ambulance service repeatedly bills Medicare for two separate ambulance transports on the same date for the same patient, with transport times that would be physically impossible given the geographic distance and documented hospital admission/discharge times. The CFE determines these services were never actually rendered. What is the primary fraud mechanism?
options: A. Inappropriate level-of-service billing
B. Fictitious billing and duplicate claim submission
C. Medically unnecessary services with proper documentation
D. Failure to obtain proper prior authorization

questions.text: A CFE investigating a clinical laboratory discovers that the facility bills Medicare for DNA-based tests (e.g., comprehensive genomic profiling) for all cancer patients, regardless of whether the tests were clinically indicated, ordered by a physician, or actually performed. The lab retains no test results or specimen documentation for 23% of billed services. Which compliance violation is most apparent?
options: A. Billing for services that lack medical necessity and/or were not rendered
B. Violating physician self-referral regulations under Stark Law provisions
C. Improper marketing of laboratory services to patients
D. Breach of laboratory CLIA certification requirements

questions.text: A hospital's CFE discovers that a cardiologist has a financial interest in a cardiac catheterization center and has increased his referral volume from 12 procedures per month to 67 procedures per month. The hospital's own cath lab utilization has decreased proportionally. No change in patient demographics or disease prevalence is documented. Under the Stark Law, what is the critical issue?
options: A. The physician is required to disclose his financial interest but the referrals are permitted if properly documented
B. The referral volume increase alone does not constitute a Stark Law violation without evidence of adverse patient outcomes
C. The physician's referrals to an entity in which he has a financial interest may violate the physician self-referral prohibition
D. The arrangement violates Anti-Kickback Statute but not Stark Law because no direct payment is made per referral

questions.text: A CFE examines a hospital's emergency department billing and identifies a pattern where patients presenting with non-emergency conditions (e.g., minor lacerations, mild gastroenteritis) are consistently billed at Level 5 Emergency Department Visit codes (CPT 99285) instead of appropriate lower levels. The medical decision-making documentation does not support the high complexity level. What is the most likely fraud indicator?
options: A. Documentation fraud involving falsification of clinical complexity
B. Systematic upcoding of ED services based on inflated medical decision-making levels
C. Billing for emergency services when patient actually received urgent care services
D. Phantom billing where ED services were documented but not rendered

questions.text: A skilled nursing facility (SNF) is under investigation by a CFE for potential Medicare fraud. The examination reveals that the facility billed for 24-hour nursing care and physical therapy services for patients classified in RUG-III group 'Ultra High' (RUH), but medical records show patients received only basic custodial care with no therapy services. The facility's quarterly therapy billing represents 34% of total Medicare claims but charts document less than 4% therapy minutes. What fraud type is most evident?
options: A. Improper DRG assignment and hospital discharge coding
B. False Resource Utilization Group (RUG) classification and therapy billing fraud
C. Violation of Conditions of Participation regarding staffing ratios
D. Improper cost reporting and depreciation allocation

questions.text: A CFE investigates a orthopedic surgeon who operates an implant supply company. The surgeon bills insurance for high-cost orthopedic implants (averaging $8,500 per implant) but purchases the same implants from manufacturers for $2,100. Markup analysis shows the surgeon's implant cost markup is 305%, significantly higher than industry standard of 45-65%. The surgeon also self-refers all orthopedic surgery patients to use his company's implants. What compliance violations are implicated?
options: A. Only Anti-Kickback Statute violation for self-referral arrangement
B. Potential Stark Law violation and possible healthcare fraud through inflated implant pricing
C. Only anti-competitive practices under federal antitrust law
D. Only tax evasion through improper business expense deductions

questions.text: A home health agency's CFE review identifies that the agency submitted claims for 60-minute skilled nursing visits to homebound patients, but GPS tracking data from nurse vehicles shows visits averaged 18-22 minutes per patient. Documentation reflects complete care plans but timestamps in electronic health records show nursing note entry times occurring after the documented visit end times. What is the primary fraud concern?
options: A. Billing for inflated/false visit durations (time fraud)
B. Providing medically unnecessary home health services
C. Improper supervision of home health aides by nurses
D. Violation of Certificate of Medical Necessity requirements

questions.text: A CFE discovers that a physical therapy clinic utilizes a standing arrangement where referring physicians receive 'marketing reimbursement' of $150 per patient referred for physical therapy. The clinic's referral sources show a concentrated pattern from three physicians whose referrals account for 67% of the clinic's revenue. When the clinic terminated the payment arrangement with one physician, referrals from that physician dropped from 23 monthly to 2 monthly within 30 days. What is the most significant compliance red flag?
options: A. Potential patient abandonment due to referral pattern disruption
B. Likely Anti-Kickback Statute violation evidenced by payment-contingent referral behavior
C. Inappropriate delegation of medical decision-making to clinic owners
D. Failure to maintain proper physician supervision of clinical staff

questions.text: A hospital's CFE analysis reveals that a radiology department has systematically billed for bilateral imaging studies (e.g., bilateral knee MRIs) when only unilateral imaging was ordered and performed. Charts show radiologist reports for only one side, but claims submitted include bilateral procedure codes with doubled technical and professional components. Over 18 months, this pattern affects 312 claims. What type of billing fraud is present?
options: A. Upcoding through misrepresentation of imaging scope
B. Bundling violation by combining separate procedures inappropriately
C. Phantom billing for procedures never performed
D. Improper modifier usage under CMS coding guidelines

questions.text: During a comprehensive fraud examination, a CFE discovers that a DME supplier has created a systematic scheme whereby family members of patients are hired as independent contractors at $25/hour for 40-hour weeks, but employee time records show zero actual work hours performed while payroll checks are distributed regularly. The supplier then bills Medicare for medical equipment 'sales consultations' provided by these contractors. What fraud mechanisms are combined in this scheme?
options: A. Money laundering and tax evasion only
B. Kickback payments disguised as employment and false claims billing
C. Improper business structure under LLC regulations
D. Violation of independent contractor classification rules under IRS standards

questions.text: A CFE is examining billing records from an orthopedic surgery center and discovers that the facility routinely bills for bilateral knee arthroscopy procedures (CPT 29881-50) on the same day for different patients, yet imaging records show only unilateral procedures were performed. The CFE also notes that operative reports are systematically altered post-hoc to match the billing codes submitted. Which fraud scheme is most evident, and what is the primary legal basis for pursuing charges?
options: A. Upcoding under the False Claims Act (31 USC 3729), as the entity knowingly presented false claims for bilateral procedures that were not performed
B. Breach of Stark Law provisions, as the facility lacks proper physician self-referral documentation
C. Violation of the Physician Payments Sunshine Act, as compensation records were not properly disclosed
D. Anti-Kickback Statute violation, as the altered reports constitute payment arrangements between the facility and surgeons

questions.text: During a healthcare fraud investigation, a CFE uncovers that a behavioral health provider submitted claims for 340 intensive outpatient program (IOP) sessions to Medicaid over 18 months, but facility surveillance footage reveals the clinic was closed (no patients present) on 187 of those billing dates. The provider claims the sessions were conducted via telehealth, but no documentation of virtual platforms, patient sign-ins, or electronic records exist. How should the CFE best classify this fraud pattern, and what quantifiable damages should be calculated?
options: A. Phantom billing; damages = 187 sessions × average reimbursement rate, plus civil penalty multiplier under False Claims Act
B. Insufficient documentation; damages = 100% refund of disputed claims only, no penalties applicable
C. Telehealth compliance violation; damages = difference between in-person and telehealth reimbursement rates
D. Billing delay issue; damages = interest on late claims submission only

questions.text: A CFE is investigating a multi-physician practice where internal analysis reveals that physician A refers 68% of eligible Medicare patients for imaging studies to a separately-owned imaging center in which A holds a 25% financial interest, compared to the 12% referral rate among the other five physicians in the practice who have no ownership stake. Physician A documents no higher clinical justification for these referrals. The practice cannot demonstrate that referrals were made in the patient's best interest rather than for financial gain. Which federal statute is primarily implicated, and what defensive position is likely unavailable?
options: A. Stark Law (42 USC 1395nn); the in-office ancillary services exception does not apply because the imaging center is not located within the referring physician's office
B. Anti-Kickback Statute; the safe harbor for physician-owned entities does not apply without proper compliance documentation
C. False Claims Act; upcoding claims because additional imaging was not medically necessary
D. Physician Payments Sunshine Act; inadequate reporting of ownership percentages to CMS

questions.text: A CFE examining a home health agency discovers that a nurse manager systematically inflated the acuity levels of 156 Medicare beneficiaries by falsifying OASIS (Outcome and Assessment Information Set) assessments, resulting in assignment to higher Home Health Resource Groups (HHRGs) and increased reimbursement. The false documentation inflated average patient acuity from the expected 3.2 severity index to 5.8, generating approximately $847,000 in overpayment. The nurse has no financial ownership in the agency. What is the most significant complicating factor for prosecution, and which remedy is most appropriate?
options: A. The employee likely qualifies for whistleblower protection under the False Claims Act, limiting direct employer liability unless management-level knowledge is demonstrated; civil recovery + treble damages is appropriate
B. The fraud is administrative only with no patient harm; therefore, only administrative suspension of Medicare privileges applies
C. Individual employee liability cannot exceed the salary component for one year of employment regardless of damages caused
D. The OASIS assessment is subjective clinical judgment; therefore, intentional falsification cannot be prosecuted as fraud

questions.text: A CFE is analyzing Medicare claims from a cardiology practice and identifies a pattern: 94 patients diagnosed with 'acute coronary syndrome' (ACS) subsequently received coronary artery stent placement within 24 hours. The CFE's comparison with 847 similar ACS patients in the same geographic region shows the practice's stent-placement rate is 3.2 standard deviations above the mean. Clinical review reveals that for 58 of these 94 patients, diagnostic evidence (ECG, troponin levels, angiography findings) did not meet established ACS criteria. Which data analytics technique is most appropriate for identifying additional fraudulent patterns, and what is the proper next investigative step?
options: A. Benford's Law analysis to identify anomalies in diagnosis code distribution; next step is clinical peer review of the 58 questionable cases by independent cardiologists
B. Regression analysis to predict expected stent placement rates; next step is immediate referral to law enforcement without clinical validation
C. Frequency distribution analysis only; next step is administrative claim denial without further investigation
D. Chi-square testing to compare procedure codes; next step is automatic licensure revocation by state medical board

questions.text: A CFE investigating a hospital system discovers that the compliance officer, during a routine audit, identified $3.2 million in questionable billing across 2,847 claims submitted over 24 months. Rather than reporting the findings to the OIG as required, the compliance officer reported only to the hospital CFO, who directed that the hospital submit a voluntary refund of $1.8 million without disclosure of the full scope of overpayment. The compliance officer was subsequently promoted. What is the primary legal consequence of this conduct, and how does it affect the hospital's liability exposure?
options: A. The hospital's failure to report to the OIG and the underdisclosed refund may constitute concealment; liability exposure potentially increases because partial disclosure and incomplete refund demonstrate conscious avoidance, potentially subjecting the hospital to treble damages under the False Claims Act
B. The voluntary refund eliminates all liability regardless of disclosure timing or completeness
C. The promotion of the compliance officer demonstrates good-faith efforts; therefore, penalties are waived
D. The OIG must be contacted only if total overpayments exceed $5 million; therefore, this conduct was compliant

questions.text: A CFE is examining a durable medical equipment (DME) supplier's billing and discovers that the supplier submitted claims for 412 diabetic testing supplies (lancets, test strips, glucose monitors) to beneficiaries who, according to Medicare enrollment records, were either deceased (47 cases), had moved to assisted living facilities where such supplies are included in facility charges (163 cases), or had explicitly requested cessation of services 6+ months prior (202 cases). The supplier's stated justification is that beneficiary contact information was outdated. Which investigative finding would most significantly strengthen a fraud charge versus a negligence finding?
options: A. Evidence that the supplier's inventory management system automatically generated and submitted refill claims without individual beneficiary verification, combined with documentation that management received complaints about duplicate supplies but failed to implement controls
B. A single instance where a beneficiary's family member called to request supplies
C. Documentation that the supplier followed manufacturer-recommended refill schedules
D. Evidence that other DME suppliers in the region submitted claims at similar rates
questions.tag: DME Fraud and Beneficiary Verification Failures

questions.text: A CFE is reviewing a physical therapy practice's records and identifies that 1,247 Medicare claims over 14 months were submitted for 'manual therapy procedures' (CPT 97161-97168), with an average of 6.8 units billed per patient visit, whereas the national average for similar practices is 2.1 units. Chart reviews indicate that treatment notes are generic, with identical language appearing across 340 patient files, and do not document the specific manual therapy techniques allegedly provided. Time-motion studies show that the documented treatment duration does not align with claimed procedure codes. What is the most likely fraud classification, and what discovery should the CFE prioritize?
options: A. Unbundling or upcoding of manual therapy procedures; prioritize acquisition of video surveillance of treatment sessions and physical examination of billing work station access logs to determine who submitted claims
B. Telehealth reimbursement violation; prioritize review of patient satisfaction surveys
C. Stark Law violation; prioritize identification of physician referral patterns
D. Anti-Kickback Statute violation; prioritize analysis of staff compensation structures
questions.tag: Procedure Code Manipulation and Documentation Fraud

questions.text: A CFE is investigating potential mental health provider fraud at a community health center and discovers that a psychiatrist submitted claims for 127 individual psychotherapy sessions (CPT 90837) on dates when facility access logs show the psychiatrist was not on-site, and when the psychiatrist was on-site, the duration of clinic hours does not accommodate the claimed number of 60-minute sessions. Furthermore, the CFE's analysis reveals that the psychiatrist's billing patterns correlate suspiciously with periods immediately following budget shortfalls reported in financial statements. The provider claims that detailed progress notes prove all services were delivered. Why might the CFE's conclusion that documentation alone proves service delivery be premature?
options: A. Documentation can be falsified after-the-fact or created fictitiously; the CFE must independently verify delivery through correlation with facility access systems, time-motion analysis, and beneficiary interviews before accepting documentation as definitive proof of service
B. Progress notes are always presumed accurate in healthcare settings
C. CFE methodology does not permit questioning clinical documentation
D. Beneficiary statements are inadmissible and cannot be used to corroborate or contradict claims
questions.tag: Mental Health Services Fraud and Service Verification

questions.text: A CFE examining a hospital's supply chain discovers that the hospital pharmacy director has negotiated 'volume discount' agreements with three pharmaceutical manufacturers contingent upon the hospital achieving specific minimum purchase volumes. The CFE identifies that antibiotics from Manufacturer A (which offers the highest discount for high-volume purchases) are prescribed at rates 47% above the hospital's historical average and 63% above regional norms for similar patient populations, despite the availability of clinically equivalent generics at substantially lower costs. Hospital quality metrics show no improvement. The pharmacy director receives a quarterly bonus based on 'cost containment savings.' Is this arrangement vulnerable to fraud liability?
options: A. Yes; if the hospital's bonus structure incentivizes prescribing decisions based on manufacturer discounts rather than clinical necessity or patient benefit, it may constitute an anti-competitive scheme or implicit kickback arrangement that circumvents legitimate physician decision-making authority
B. No; volume discounts are standard in healthcare and cannot constitute fraud
C. No; hospital-negotiated contracts are exempt from fraud statutes
D. Yes; any prescription above regional average automatically constitutes fraud regardless of clinical factors

questions.text: A CFE is investigating a hospital's use of contract temporary staffing agencies for nursing positions and discovers that the hospital routinely bills Medicare for 'physician supervision' of nursing care and 'hospital-provided' services when, in fact, the nurses are employed by staffing agencies and paid by those agencies, not by the hospital. The hospital's markup on staffing costs is 23% above what it would be for directly-employed staff. The CFE also discovers that the hospital never actually provides the supervisory oversight it bills for. Which federal reimbursement principle is violated, and what is the basis for liability?
options: A. The principle of actual provision of services; the hospital is billing for services (physician supervision and hospital nursing care) that it did not actually provide, and falsely representing staffing arrangements to CMS, violating the False Claims Act
B. The hospital is permitted to bill for contracted services under Medicare's contractor billing rules without limitation
C. Staffing arrangement changes do not affect billing methodology
D. Only the staffing agency can be held liable, not the hospital

questions.text: A CFE is conducting a comprehensive fraud risk assessment for a large medical device manufacturer and identifies that the company has structured its sales representative compensation to include bonuses tied to unit sales volume to specific medical practices, with no corresponding requirement that purchase volumes align with actual patient usage or legitimate clinical need. The CFE's analysis of purchasing patterns reveals that one cardiac surgery practice increased device purchases from 156 units annually (pre-bonus structure) to 847 units annually (post-bonus structure) without corresponding increases in case volumes or patient populations served. The practice's implantation failure rates increased from 2.1% to 5.8%. What compliance violation is evident, and what is the required remedial action?
options: A. Anti-Kickback Statute violation through volume-based compensation contingent upon referrals; the manufacturer must restructure compensation to remove unit-sales contingency, implement controls ensuring purchases align with documented clinical need, and potentially conduct a government-requested corrective action plan including monitoring
B. No violation; manufacturers may offer volume discounts without restriction
C. Only a state-level licensing violation requiring notification to state authorities
D. A civil contract dispute between manufacturer and practice only; no healthcare fraud statute applies
questions.tag: Device Manufacturer Fraud and Sales Inducements

questions.text: A CFE is reviewing a hospital system's coding practices and discovers that 34% of inpatient claims include a diagnosis code for 'hospital-acquired infection' (HAI) (secondary diagnosis codes in the 996-999 range), compared to 6% at peer hospitals in the same geographic region. The CFE's review of medical records reveals that while documentation mentions 'infection risk' or 'infection precautions,' the clinical evidence does not support actual diagnosis of a hospital-acquired infection in 89% of the flagged cases. These secondary diagnoses result in DRG severity adjustments increasing reimbursement by an average of $8,400 per case. A hospital administrator acknowledges the pattern and states: 'Our documentation is more complete than other hospitals.' Is this explanation consistent with fraud detection findings?
options: A. No; when secondary diagnosis rates are statistical outliers and chart review reveals that documented clinical evidence does not support the coded diagnoses in the majority of cases, this pattern suggests diagnosis creep or upcoding rather than superior documentation, regardless of the administrator's characterization
B. Yes; hospitals with better documentation practices naturally have higher secondary diagnosis rates
C. No; statistical outliers are always caused by data entry errors requiring automatic claim correction
D. Yes; peer hospital comparison data cannot be used in fraud investigations
questions.tag: Diagnosis Code Creep and Secondary Diagnosis Inflation
